hacks.title: Chromotone
hacks.description: Exploring multiwavelength astronomy with sound. Each waveband has a pitch, the intensity/brightness in that waveband is represented by volume/loudness of the note. When you scan a mouse of an image Chromotone plays a chord with different components which correspond to the intensity at each pixel superposed. The original version was written in Processing which is not particularly web friendly so this project aims to make a version which is lighter, more efficient and has added functionality.

hacks.title: Pluto the Previous Planet
hacks.description: A song about Pluto's demotion from planetary status, performed by Amanda Bauer and collaborators at the .Astronomy 3 hack day. Covered by Universe Today.

hacks.title: AstroScanr
hacks.description: A visualisation of astronomy journal titles and abstracts using natural language processing techniques.

hacks.title: Astroworld
hacks.description: Collect information about astronomical facilities around the world, including old, abandoned observatories, flashy new ones, future ones, and archeo-astronomical sites, using EpiCollect.

hacks.title: Cosmology Calculator OS X Widget
hacks.description: A port of Ned Wright's Cosmology Calculator to an OS X widget.

hacks.title: Flora
hacks.description: Flora is an attempt to enable quantitative measurements on images in a browser. In astronomy, we want to measure the position, size and brightness of various objects in our images - which in practice means fitting a model to them. Flora uses Javascript and canvas to allow the user to point at an object they want to measure, and have a catalog entry of measurements returned. We use the astrojs library to handle FITS images (including calibration information in the header) and the transformations needed to turn pixel positions into "world" coordinates on the sky.

hacks.title: Science: It's Universal
hacks.description: An outreach video about what science is and what it is like to be a scientist.

hacks.title: Sh*t Scientists Say
hacks.description: Sh*t Astronomer Says is a collection of what astronomers love, hate and repeat.

hacks.title: Zoonibot
hacks.description: Zoonibot was conceived as a sort-of “Planet Hunters butler,” for Talk able and ready to automatically answer questions and provide detail when users request information.

hacks.title: D3PO
hacks.description: D3PO is a project designed to allow an astronomer, with no specialized data visualization skills, to make an interactive, publication-quality figure that has staged builds and linked brushing across plots.

hacks.title: Don't Call Me Colin
hacks.description: The International Astronomical Union has announced that extrasolar planets might get more interesting names. This is making one planet, Gliese 581d, a bit angsty. It really wants a nice mythical name like the Solar System planets and not to be named after celebrities or you mum or your cat (Colin).

hacks.title: Python Client for the NASA ADS API
hacks.description: A Python module to interact with NASA's ADS that Doesn't Suck.

hacks.title: AAS Job Viz
hacks.description: A visualisation of the AAS job register using location to pin jobs on a map. Allows users to filter jobs by type and location. This project uses the Leaflet.js Javascript library.

hacks.title: Adler Zodiac Story
hacks.description: Online storytelling connecting the Chinese Zodiac to astronomy

hacks.title: Astronomer Bot (BotAstro)
hacks.description: A Twitter bot for astronomers.

hacks.title: Cite Me Maybe
hacks.description: See which articles were referenced in your "field" that a paper did not cite.

hacks.title: Elevate ApJ
hacks.description: Working to elevate the readability and design of astronomy journal articles.

hacks.title: Kepler Song and Drum Machine
hacks.description: Making music from Kepler light curves

hacks.title: Rate My Institution
hacks.description: A way to honestly rate your place of academic work

hacks.title: Tones and Drones: Sampling Stars
hacks.description: Sonifying Kepler light curves to create music.

hacks.title: Unclockify
hacks.description: Chrome extension to find and replace sexigesimal coordinates on Wikipedia

hacks.title: Life Cycle of Astronomers
hacks.description: Visualising astronomy career paths with D3.js.

hacks.title: Literal Virtual Observatory
hacks.description: Drive around telescope observatories in this Mad Max/gamer-style interactive visualisation. Demo available on USB.

hacks.title: Pudding to the Max
hacks.description: Catch up with the exploits of the Cosmic Pudding at #dotastro 8 through Koalas to the Max. Who will you get?

hacks.title: Radio Yourself
hacks.description: Upload an image and see what you'd look like through a radio telescope.

hacks.title: Space Shark
hacks.description: An alt-az pointer to be used for education.

hacks.title: What's Up?
hacks.description: Updated Astroplan web app to help you plan observations.

hacks.title: robo-ph
hacks.description: A podcast which reads you astro-ph abstract by abstract.

hacks.title: webcite
hacks.description: This hack takes an ADS bibliographic code and plots a geographical web of citations for that paper. It finds the institutions of the first authors of papers which cite the given paper. You can also toggle to show the web for all the authors on the papers which cite the given paper. It uses leaflet to display the map data.

hacks.title: .draft
hacks.description: Automagically highlight LaTeX differences between GitHub drafts.

hacks.title: Are You Still Talking?
hacks.description: Use a blink(1) light to control your speakers when you are chairing a session. This is a python package which installs a command line talk timer. By entering a total talk time and warning period, you will start the blink(1) light glowing green while they talking. It changes to orange when they are in the warning period, then red when their time is up. If they continue talking the light flashes a headache inducing light show until you control-C.

hacks.title: AstroBadger
hacks.description: Getting you in the habit of writing 200 academic words per day, every day. Inspired by 750words.com. Gamified writing! Chrome plugin + Flask app.

hacks.title: AstroGoldStars
hacks.description: Give a Gold Star to a fellow scientist by tweeting @username++ at @AstroGoldStars on Twitter. Solves credit sharing in science.

hacks.title: Fakespeare
hacks.description: A LSTM neural network trained on the corpus of all Shakespeare plays that can generate hilarious and nonsensical plays.

hacks.title: Galaxy Zoo Maze
hacks.description: Creates a responsive view of a Zooniverse subject and a QR code, then supports scanning and reacting to result compared to local data file.

hacks.title: GitNL
hacks.description: Run Git commands using normal English sentences parsed with Google's natural language processor.

hacks.title: Hacks Collector
hacks.description: A way to archive all hacks made at .Astronomy conferences. Each hack has a separate YML file containing all meta data. The YAML files are validated and we then auto-build a new version of the website with the new hack listed.

hacks.title: Markov Papers
hacks.description: This hack generates paper titles/abstracts with a Markov chain trained on real papers returned by any ADS search keywords.

hacks.title: Meta^4hack
hacks.description: Metadata on a hack about metadata on hacks. I.e., the submission form for this hack list.

hacks.title: Project Apollo Stories
hacks.description: A Zooniverse Project: Help astronomers to re-create the incredible stories from the Apollo missions. Takes user selected images from the Apollo Project Flickr image gallery and creates beautiful Timeline JS interactive stories

hacks.title: SIMBAD the Bot
hacks.description: Twitter bot which tweets a multi-wavelength animated GIF of a random SIMBAD object a few times a day. Serendipitously explore the sky!

hacks.title: Spacebar
hacks.description: Can you write the fastest paper? Keep rain out of the dome while observing? Find the galaxies? Show off your skills with these astronomy themed mini-games. As we're all busy people, you only need to master one button to do so... the spacebar. Disclaimer - work-in-progress; no actual win condition; games not guaranteed to be fun.

hacks.title: Stellar Autopsies Zooniverse
hacks.description: A Zooniverse Project: Stellar Autopsies to classify the light curves of dying stars.

hacks.title: StingRA: Simbad Quick-Look Chrome Extension
hacks.description: A Chrome extension that lets astronomers highlight an astronomical object and open a quick-look pop-up of magnitudes, images, sky position, and links to SIMBAD and WorldWide Telescope.

hacks.title: The ArXiv Mailer
hacks.description: An app to modernise the formatting of the daily arXiv email.

hacks.title: arxiv59
hacks.description: Twitter bot that posts papers which missed the top-of-the-arXiv deadline by about a second.

hacks.title: bvi-sonification
hacks.description: Uses a Leap Motion device to allow a user to listen to a galaxy's spectra based on hand position.

hacks.title: Hack Graveyard
hacks.description: dotAstronomy is full of amazing pitches and amazing hacks. Unfortunately, we do not have enough hours in the "Hack Day" to complete all of our hacks. The "Hack Graveyard" keeps track of these hacks so they can be revived in the future!

hacks.title: Hack Roulette
hacks.description: Spin the wheel to generate a new hack using past dotastro hacks.

hacks.title: Project PHaEDRA Website and Logo
hacks.description: Rethinking/rough drafting a logo and website for Project PHaEDRA: a collaborative effort to digitize and transcribe the work of early astronomers.

hacks.title: Sketch dotAstro: Hunting for Jupiter Sketches in Archival Notebooks
hacks.description: Attempting to use computer vision to distinguish between boring pages in archival notebooks and pages with sketches of Jupiter.

hacks.title: Wave At Kepler
hacks.description: Static website to compute the direction to Kepler when it takes a photo, so you can wave at the right moment.

hacks.title: Where in the Universe?
hacks.description: Have you ever wondered where you'd currently be in the Universe if you were launched towards the zenith above your birth place at the time you were born? Well we hacked a simple site to calculate your position in space and determine the nearest star to you and its distance.

hacks.title: Whose Line Is It Anyway?
hacks.description: Have you ever plotted a 1D spectrum and wondered what a particular absorption/emission feature was? `whoseline` is a small package for plotting common spectral line lists on your spectra, so you can discover whose line is responsible for a particular absorption/emission line.

hacks.title: xorastro: Extremely Open, Extremely Reproducible Astronomy
hacks.description: Built a neural network to predict galaxy colors from galaxy shapes in the most open and reproducible way possible.

hacks.title: .Astronomy X Resources
hacks.description: Repository for information and materials from .Astronomy X.

hacks.title: .Astronomy X Scripts
hacks.description: Scripts and code developed at .Astronomy X.

hacks.title: Contributor Plots for Astropy-Affiliated Packages
hacks.description: Code that uses GitHub REST API to plot the number of unique contributors over time for astopy-affiliated packages

hacks.title: Ru-stronomy
hacks.description: A Markov chain generator trained on drag queen names, astronomical acronyms, RuPaul lyrics, and clickbait science headlines. Generates new astronomical mission names that sound like drag queens -- and vice versa. Created as part of the "Engaging astronomers in the era of clickbait" talk.

hacks.title: Contributing to Astropy
hacks.description: A session on how to contribute to the Astropy project.

hacks.title: Multi-Order Coverage (MOC) Hack
hacks.description: Working with Multi-Order Coverage maps for astronomical footprints in MAST.

hacks.title: Snakemake and Show Your Work
hacks.description: A tutorial session on reproducible workflows using Snakemake and the showyourwork framework.

hacks.title: dotAstro Demo
hacks.description: Demo materials from .Astronomy 12.

hacks.title: Adding to the ESASky Virtual Assistant
Astronomy as a Great Tool to Have Impact on Humans in 21st Century
Getting Started with ML in Astronomy
How to Tailor Outreach Projects for Marginalised Communities
Promoting Bluesky to Astronomers
hacks.description: A live feed project connected to the conference, promoting Bluesky as a social platform for astronomers.

hacks.title: Redo the Software Use in Astro Survey
Set up JDaviz Example Notebooks on Datalabs
So You Want to Be an Astropy Contributor?
